profile.name: Temitayo Charles Akinniranye
profile.title: Founder · Platform Engineering · AI Infrastructure
profile.tagline: I build reliable cloud platforms, private AI workflows, and operator-friendly automation for teams that need production discipline without enterprise bloat.

aboutParagraphs: I build practical infrastructure: Kubernetes platforms, GitOps delivery paths, secure secret workflows, private AI systems, and deployment pipelines that are understandable enough to operate under pressure.

This portfolio is not just a landing page. It is connected to a real Home Lab deployment path, a FastAPI backend, CI checks, container scanning, browser smoke tests, and structured content served through an API.
My focus is useful engineering: clean systems, clear runbooks, reliable recovery paths, and automation that helps operators make better decisions instead of hiding complexity behind dashboards.

aboutHighlights.label: Platform-first
aboutHighlights.value: Kubernetes, GitOps, networking, observability, and recovery designed as one operating model.

aboutHighlights.label: AI with control
aboutHighlights.value: Private assistant workflows with explicit memory, approval gates, and operator ownership.

aboutHighlights.label: Deployment discipline
aboutHighlights.value: Small, reviewable changes backed by CI, smoke tests, image scanning, and rollback-aware delivery.

aboutHighlights.label: Business-aware
aboutHighlights.value: Infrastructure choices explained in terms of reliability, cost, risk, and delivery speed.

experiences.summary: Architect and operate cloud platform, CI/CD, Kubernetes, GitOps, DevSecOps, observability, and reliability workflows for production environments, improving deployment speed, infrastructure reliability, cost governance, and operational consistency.
achievements: Reduced deployment time from 4 hours to 15 minutes via GitHub Actions and Jenkins pipelines.
Enabled 200+ monthly production releases with zero rollbacks through stronger validation and delivery controls.
Lifted platform reliability from 99.9% to 99.99% for infra serving 2M+ API requests / day.
Cut AWS spend by ~$18K / month through audits, rightsizing, tagging, and governance.
Drove GitOps adoption with ArgoCD and Helm, 40+ production releases / week, no weekend windows.
Integrated Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and HashiCorp Vault into delivery for DevSecOps and audit readiness.
Designed disaster recovery across 8 services, RTO 4h → 15m, MTTR < 30 minutes.

experiences.summary: Modernized application delivery, cloud infrastructure, automation, monitoring, and operational support for AWS-based environments, supporting cloud migration, microservices delivery, secure infra design, and 24/7 platform operations.
achievements: Led migration of 12 monolithic applications to AWS, 35% infrastructure cost reduction.
Designed GitLab CI/CD workflows scaling releases from weekly to multiple daily pushes.
Built multi-AZ VPCs, least-privilege IAM, and HA EKS clusters with Terraform.
Automated provisioning, patching, and rollouts via Ansible + Terraform, env build days → < 4 hours.
Created Bash / Python automation for backups, restoration, and log management, 40% less manual work.
Established centralized observability with ELK + CloudWatch, 35% lower MTTD on critical incidents.

experiences.summary: Developed and maintained CI/CD pipelines, infrastructure automation, containerized workloads, monitoring systems, and secure AWS operations, strengthening foundations in repeatable delivery and cloud reliability.
achievements: Built CI/CD and infrastructure automation using GitLab CI/CD, Terraform, and Ansible.
Implemented Docker and Kubernetes / EKS practices for scalability and deployment consistency.
Created automated monitoring and logging workflows with ELK Stack and CloudWatch.
Enforced IAM best practices and managed secrets via AWS SSM Parameter Store.

experiences.role: Linux Systems Engineer · Scrum Facilitator · Linux SysAdmin
experiences.company: Beyond Solutions · British Council Bahrain · Cambridge International School
experiences.summary: Managed Linux systems, server operations, backups, monitoring, storage, Apache, MySQL, and infrastructure documentation, building the operational discipline that still shapes how I design reliable platforms today.
achievements: Managed and optimized 200+ Ubuntu and CentOS servers.
Automated backups, log rotation, maintenance, and monitoring with Bash and Python.
Authored technical runbooks and disaster recovery documentation.

projects.name: TCA InfraForge
projects.subtitle: My Platform Engineering Company
projects.description: TCA InfraForge is the platform engineering company I own and operate. It packages Kubernetes operations, GitOps delivery, secrets management, private networking, observability, and recovery workflows into a practical infrastructure model for small teams that need production discipline without building a full platform department.

diagramImage.alt: TCA InfraForge company platform architecture diagram

caseStudy.title: How I operate InfraForge as a company platform
caseStudy.context: InfraForge is the business wrapper around my platform engineering work. The goal is to give startups and operators a reliable infrastructure baseline that covers delivery, secrets, routing, observability, backups, recovery, and operational runbooks.
decisions: Use GitOps as the operating source of truth so infrastructure changes are auditable and repeatable.
Separate application delivery from gateway and networking ownership to avoid Argo CD shared-resource drift.
Keep stateful services recoverable through Longhorn retention, Harbor recovery notes, and documented restore paths.
caseStudy.result: The platform now supports production domain routing, Harbor registry recovery, portfolio API delivery, Vault-backed secrets, and a hardened operating model that can be reused for company and client workloads.

projects.name: Project Iris
projects.subtitle: Private AI Workspace for Knowledge Operators
projects.description: Project Iris is a private AI workspace for operators who need useful automation without surrendering control of memory, credentials, browser actions, or internal context.

diagramImage.alt: Project Iris local-first AI workspace architecture diagram

caseStudy.title: Private AI operations for founders and technical teams
caseStudy.context: Project Iris explores how a private AI workspace can support operators without giving away control of memory, tools, credentials, or execution boundaries.
decisions: Treat memory as governed infrastructure instead of a hidden black box.
Keep tool execution explicit so users understand what the assistant can act on.
Design for TLS, auth, backup, and recovery from the beginning instead of after the prototype phase.
caseStudy.result: The project demonstrates how AI products can feel useful while still respecting operational boundaries, privacy needs, and founder-level control.

projects.name: Jerry
projects.subtitle: Durable Personal AI Assistant
projects.description: Jerry is a local-first personal assistant system built around durable memory, guarded actions, private context, and recoverable state. It treats personal AI as owned infrastructure, not a disposable chatbot.

caseStudy.title: A personal assistant designed like infrastructure
caseStudy.context: Jerry explores what happens when a personal AI assistant is treated as an operating system for daily work rather than a disposable chatbot. The focus is durable memory, explicit consent, and recoverable state.
decisions: Use local-first storage and model execution to keep personal context under the operator's control.
Route meaningful actions through approval gates so the assistant can help without becoming opaque or risky.
Treat memory, backups, and audit history as first-class parts of the assistant architecture.
caseStudy.result: The system demonstrates a premium personal AI pattern: private by default, durable over time, and designed for controlled action instead of uncontrolled automation.

projects.name: AI Builders Academy
projects.subtitle: Program Delivery Platform for AI Education
projects.description: AI Builders Academy connects an education product to the operational pieces that make it deployable: application paths, image promotion, GitOps release controls, runtime checks, and runbooks.

caseStudy.title: A business delivery platform for AI education
caseStudy.context: AI Builders Academy needs delivery discipline that fits a real education business: fast application changes, clear deployable boundaries, and runtime confidence.
decisions: Refine deployable path filters so only relevant changes trigger product delivery workflows.
Promote runtime images deliberately instead of relying on ambiguous moving state.
Add drift checks and runbooks so platform behavior stays visible after deploy.
caseStudy.result: The platform is positioned as an operating system for an AI education business, not a one-off app build.

projects.name: Vault Ops Toolkit
projects.subtitle: Secrets Operations for Platform Teams
projects.description: Vault Ops Toolkit helps operators handle secrets safely across environments with Vault-backed workflows, Kubernetes External Secrets validation, bootstrap automation, and practical recovery procedures.

caseStudy.title: Secrets operations that teams can safely run
caseStudy.context: Vault Ops exists for platform teams that need secure secret workflows without turning every rotation, sync, or recovery step into risky manual work.
decisions: Keep Vault as the authority while External Secrets handles Kubernetes delivery.
Document bootstrap and recovery paths so operators can act under pressure.
Validate secret shape and sync state before application rollouts depend on them.
caseStudy.result: The toolkit gives platform work a safer secrets operating model that is audit-friendly and practical for small teams.

projects.name: ForgeWatch
projects.subtitle: Evidence-First AIOps Copilot for InfraForge
projects.description: ForgeWatch is an evidence-first AIOps copilot for InfraForge. It gathers signals from Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD, then turns alerts into structured triage with human-approved remediation.

caseStudy.title: Evidence-first operations for lean platform teams
caseStudy.context: ForgeWatch is the operational intelligence layer for InfraForge. It uses tool calling to collect real platform evidence first, then produces triage, digests, watchdog findings, and approval-gated write proposals for operators.
decisions: Keep Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD queries autonomous but read-only by default.
Route all mutating actions through propose_write, policy evaluation, and explicit operator approval.
Store outcomes and incident memory so future triage improves without turning the system into an unsafe autonomous executor.
caseStudy.result: ForgeWatch gives InfraForge a company-grade AIOps control plane where AI speeds investigation, but operators retain authority over production state.

projects.name: OpenLeaf Reader Platform
projects.subtitle: Reference SaaS Architecture for Digital Products
projects.description: A reference SaaS platform for digital content businesses, modeling API gateway governance, event-driven service boundaries, payments integration, release automation, observability, and reliability testing.

caseStudy.title: A SaaS architecture reference for digital products
caseStudy.context: OpenLeaf models the architecture patterns a digital content business needs once product delivery moves beyond a simple monolith.
decisions: Use gateway governance to keep APIs discoverable and controlled.
Model event-driven boundaries where services need asynchronous coordination.
Include observability and chaos testing so reliability is visible, not assumed.
caseStudy.result: The project serves as a public architecture reference for product teams thinking through cloud-native SaaS design.

projectArchitectures.label: Company platform
projectArchitectures.title: TCA InfraForge operating platform
projectArchitectures.summary: The company platform that packages Kubernetes delivery, GitOps, secrets, private networking, observability, registry recovery, and disaster recovery into a repeatable operating system for startups and growing teams.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows that InfraForge is a real platform company with an owned operating model, not a collection of lab resources.
projectArchitectures.metric: GitOps, secrets, registry, DR, and edge routing

nodes.label: Cloudflare
nodes.details: Public DNS, TLS edge, caching, and routed entry for company and client-facing endpoints.

nodes.label: Tailscale
nodes.details: Private operator access path for internal systems and administrative workflows.

nodes.label: Envoy Gateway
nodes.details: Gateway API routing for internal products, Harbor, portfolio API, and platform services.

nodes.label: Argo CD
nodes.details: GitOps reconciler that applies platform applications, sync waves, and ownership boundaries.

nodes.label: Vault
nodes.details: Secrets authority for platform and workload credentials.

nodes.label: External Secrets
nodes.details: Kubernetes bridge that syncs approved secret values into application namespaces.

nodes.label: Workloads
nodes.details: Portfolio API, AI Builders, ForgeWatch, platform services, and client-ready workloads.

nodes.label: Harbor
nodes.details: Private container registry with retained Longhorn storage and recovery runbooks.

nodes.label: Longhorn
nodes.details: Persistent volume layer for registry, databases, and other stateful services.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.details: Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, Alertmanager, Netdata, and runbooks for operational visibility.

flows: Git commit updates the platform repository, then Argo CD reconciles the desired state into the cluster.
Cloudflare sends public traffic to Envoy Gateway, then Gateway API routes requests to the correct namespace service.
Vault remains the source of truth for credentials while External Secrets delivers only approved runtime values.
Longhorn retained volumes and runbooks make stateful recovery practical after namespace or pod failure.

projectArchitectures.label: Private AI workspace
projectArchitectures.title: Project Iris governed AI workspace
projectArchitectures.summary: A local-first AI workspace for founders and operators that treats memory, browser actions, tool use, TLS, backup, and identity as governed infrastructure.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Positions private AI as a controlled product workspace for teams that need capability without losing custody of data and actions.
projectArchitectures.metric: Private memory, tool control, and recovery

nodes.label: Operator
nodes.details: Founder, operator, or technical user working inside a private AI workspace.

nodes.label: React UI
nodes.details: Workspace interface for conversations, workflows, managed actions, and project context.

nodes.label: FastAPI
nodes.details: Backend boundary for auth, memory operations, tool routing, and workflow state.

nodes.label: Auth + TLS
nodes.details: Identity and transport controls so the workspace is not an open local demo.

nodes.label: Memory Store
nodes.details: Governed long-term memory with explicit user control and backup awareness.

nodes.label: Tool Router
nodes.details: Explicit tool execution boundary for browser actions, local actions, and workflow helpers.

nodes.label: Managed Browser
nodes.details: Controlled browser action path for interactive tasks that should remain visible and governable.

nodes.label: Backups
nodes.details: Backup and restore path for workspace state, memory, and configuration.

flows: The user interacts through a private UI, not an uncontrolled public assistant surface.
The API checks auth and routes memory or tool requests through explicit backend boundaries.
Tool execution remains visible and separated from memory so users understand what the assistant can do.
Backups protect workspace state so the system can be recovered after local or runtime failure.

projectArchitectures.label: Personal AI system
projectArchitectures.title: Jerry durable personal assistant architecture
projectArchitectures.summary: Jerry is modeled as a local-first personal assistant with durable memory, retrieval, guarded actions, and backup-aware state. The architecture frames the assistant as an owned operating system for personal productivity rather than a novelty chatbot.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how personal AI can be designed with the same discipline as production software: memory, consent, auditability, durability, and reversible execution.
projectArchitectures.metric: Durable memory, local-first control, guarded execution

nodes.label: Input Channels
nodes.details: Chat, notes, local files, browser context, images, and other user-controlled sources.

nodes.label: Assistant Core
nodes.details: Intent routing, task continuity, conversation state, reminders, and personal workflow coordination.

nodes.label: Local Models
nodes.details: Local model runtime and embeddings support private reasoning, retrieval, and summarization.

nodes.label: Semantic Memory
nodes.details: Durable memory treats personal context as governed infrastructure rather than transient chat history.

nodes.label: Guarded Actions
nodes.details: File operations, browser tasks, notifications, and sync flows pass through consent-aware execution boundaries.

nodes.label: SQLite + Backups
nodes.details: Primary local state with backup and replication paths for continuity after device or runtime failure.

nodes.label: Audit Trail
nodes.details: Actions remain reviewable and reversible so the assistant earns trust through visibility.

flows: User context enters through explicit channels so the assistant does not blur private sources with uncontrolled automation.
The assistant core routes intent through local models, semantic memory, and task continuity before recommending or taking action.
Any meaningful action crosses a guarded execution boundary with consent, logging, and reviewable outcomes.
SQLite and backup paths make memory durable enough to support long-running personal workflows.

projectArchitectures.label: Education business platform
projectArchitectures.title: AI Builders Academy delivery system
projectArchitectures.summary: A business delivery platform for AI education, with app changes, image promotion, GitOps, runtime checks, and operational runbooks tied into one product release lane.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how an education business can move quickly while keeping deployable boundaries, runtime state, and platform ownership visible.
projectArchitectures.metric: Deployable paths, image promotion, drift checks

nodes.label: Program Content
nodes.details: Curriculum, program assets, and delivery material for AI education products.

nodes.label: Frontend
nodes.details: Student and buyer-facing application interface.

nodes.label: Backend API
nodes.details: Business logic, enrollment workflows, admin operations, and product APIs.

nodes.label: CI Path Filters
nodes.details: Refined deployable path filters prevent unrelated changes from triggering unsafe releases.

nodes.label: Image Registry
nodes.details: Runtime images are promoted deliberately instead of relying on ambiguous state.

nodes.label: GitOps Release
nodes.details: Argo CD applies the promoted release into the target environment.

nodes.label: Runtime Checks
nodes.details: Drift checks and smoke validation keep deployed behavior visible.

nodes.label: Runbooks
nodes.details: Operational notes describe recovery, deployment, and verification procedures.

flows: Relevant code paths trigger the correct build lane and avoid noisy unrelated deployment activity.
Images are built, promoted, and reconciled through GitOps so release state is reviewable.
Runtime drift checks verify that the deployed product matches the expected operating state.
Runbooks document recovery and validation so the business can keep operating after changes.

projectArchitectures.label: Secrets operations
projectArchitectures.title: Vault Ops secrets control plane
projectArchitectures.summary: A secrets operations toolkit for Vault-backed platform teams that need bootstrap, rotation, External Secrets sync, validation, and recovery procedures.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Turns secrets management into a runbook-backed platform capability that teams can operate safely under pressure.

nodes.label: Platform Operator
nodes.details: Authorized operator who bootstraps, validates, and rotates platform secrets.

nodes.label: Vault
nodes.details: System of record for sensitive credentials and environment values.

nodes.label: Policies
nodes.details: Access policy and path structure that defines who can read or write secret material.

nodes.label: External Secrets
nodes.details: Controller that syncs selected Vault values into Kubernetes secrets.

nodes.label: K8s Secrets
nodes.details: Namespace-local runtime secret objects consumed by workloads.

nodes.label: Workloads
nodes.details: Applications that consume secrets without direct Vault coupling.

nodes.label: Validation
nodes.details: Shape checks, sync checks, and recovery evidence before rollout dependency.

flows: Operators place credentials in Vault under known paths and policy boundaries.
External Secrets syncs approved values to Kubernetes namespaces without exposing Vault broadly.
Workloads consume Kubernetes secrets while audit checks confirm the secret shape before rollout.
Recovery procedures restore missing or broken secrets from the authority source instead of guessing values.

projectArchitectures.label: AIOps copilot
projectArchitectures.title: ForgeWatch evidence-first operations copilot
projectArchitectures.summary: ForgeWatch uses Ollama function calling to gather read-only evidence from Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD. It can propose write actions, but policy and operator approval control every mutation.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Gives InfraForge and lean platform teams a safer AIOps loop where AI accelerates diagnosis without silently changing production.

nodes.label: Operator
nodes.details: Engineer or platform owner asking questions, reviewing evidence, and approving or denying proposed actions.

nodes.label: ForgeWatch API
nodes.details: FastAPI service exposing ask, alert, digest, watchdog, outcome, memory, and writes routes.

nodes.label: Ollama Loop
nodes.details: Six-round tool-calling loop that decides which evidence sources to query before answering.

nodes.label: Tool Executor
nodes.details: Runs read-only tools immediately and gates write proposals through policy and approval.

nodes.label: Evidence Sources
nodes.details: Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, and Argo CD clients provide real-time platform evidence.

nodes.label: RAG Memory
nodes.details: ChromaDB, outcomes, history, and runbooks improve future triage with confirmed context.

nodes.label: Policy Engine
nodes.details: Classifies write intent, blocks dangerous commands, and forces approval for mutating actions.

nodes.label: Discord / ntfy
nodes.details: Optional proactive notifications for digest, watchdog, and startup status.

flows: An operator or alert calls the API with a platform question or incident payload.
Ollama selects tools and the executor queries Kubernetes, Prometheus, Loki, or Argo CD for evidence.
If remediation is needed, ForgeWatch creates a write proposal instead of executing it automatically.
Policy evaluates the command, then the operator approves or denies through the writes endpoint.
Outcomes and incident notes feed memory so future responses improve with confirmed operational context.

projectArchitectures.label: SaaS reference
projectArchitectures.title: OpenLeaf digital product platform
projectArchitectures.summary: A reference SaaS architecture for digital content products with API gateway governance, service boundaries, async messaging, payments, observability, GitOps, and reliability testing.
projectArchitectures.businessValue: Shows how a digital product company can evolve from app code into service ownership, gateway control, and reliability practice.

nodes.label: Reader UI
nodes.details: Customer-facing reading and content experience.

nodes.label: Kong Gateway
nodes.details: API gateway governance with route, policy, and service exposure control.

nodes.label: User Service
nodes.details: Identity and user profile boundary.

nodes.label: Book Service
nodes.details: Content catalog and reader domain boundary.

nodes.label: Payments
nodes.details: Stripe or payment provider integration for subscriptions and transactions.

nodes.label: RabbitMQ
nodes.details: Asynchronous coordination for product events and service communication.

nodes.label: MongoDB
nodes.details: Document data store for reader and product state.

nodes.label: Prometheus + Chaos
nodes.details: Metrics and reliability testing make service behavior visible under stress.

flows: Readers use the frontend, while Kong keeps service APIs governed through one controlled entry point.
User, catalog, and billing services own separate business boundaries rather than one tangled app surface.
RabbitMQ carries async events so product actions can be processed without coupling every service directly.
Prometheus and chaos testing expose reliability behavior before customers experience hidden failure modes.

writings.title: Cutting a 4-Hour Deploy to 15 Minutes: A GitOps Playbook
writings.excerpt: How we replaced a brittle weekly release train with ArgoCD, reusable Helm charts, and automated release validation. Covers reusable pipeline primitives, rollout strategy, and the quality gates that let us ship 40+ production releases per week.
writings.noteTitle: How I design Kubernetes platforms for companies
writings.noteBody: The goal is not to collect Kubernetes tools. The goal is to give a company a stable operating model: Git as the source of truth, a gateway layer that owns ingress clearly, a secrets path that operators can audit, observability that explains failures, and recovery procedures that are tested before they are needed.

writings.title: Shaving $216K a Year Off AWS Without Breaking Production
writings.excerpt: A practical FinOps walkthrough: tagging strategy, rightsizing cadence, savings-plan bets, idle compute hunting, and how governance prevented regressions at 2M+ API requests per day.
writings.noteBody: AI operations should not mean blind automation. I design the flow so telemetry, logs, runbooks, and prior incidents create context first. The assistant proposes ranked actions, but the operator keeps approval authority. That keeps AI useful without giving it unsafe production control.

writings.title: Designing AIOps with Human-in-the-Loop Safety
writings.excerpt: Why evidence-grounded triage, runbook memory, and read-only analysis are the default posture before you let LLMs near production. A deep dive into the ForgeWatch approach.
writings.noteBody: GitOps works best when teams treat it as a control system, not just a deploy button. Ownership boundaries, sync waves, rollback paths, and drift checks matter because they prevent platform teams from debugging invisible state during outages.

writings.title: 99.99% Isn't a Metric, It's a Culture
writings.excerpt: From error budgets and SLOs to on-call hygiene and blameless postmortems. How platform discipline, not heroics, gets you the extra nine.
writings.noteBody: Secrets work should be boring, auditable, and recoverable. Vault, External Secrets, and clear runbooks let a team rotate credentials, recover missing values, and validate application configuration without guessing under pressure.

writings.title: Bare-Metal Kubernetes That Feels Like Production
writings.excerpt: Inside TCA InfraForge: Ubuntu + K3s, Gateway API via Envoy, Longhorn storage, External Secrets with Vault, Velero backups, and a Kyverno policy stack that mirrors enterprise controls.
writings.noteBody: Recovery is only real when operators know which data matters, where it lives, how to restore it, and how to prove the application works after the restore. For stateful Kubernetes services, volume retention, PVC binding, and app-level validation all matter.

writings.title: Supply Chain Security on a Delivery Team's Budget
writings.excerpt: How to fold Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and Vault-backed secrets into an existing CI/CD pipeline without slowing down the team or turning security into a bottleneck.
writings.noteTitle: Supply chain security without blocking delivery
writings.noteBody: Security checks become useful when they are placed where developers already work. Trivy, Checkov, SonarQube, and Vault-backed secrets can improve confidence without becoming release theater if findings are visible, prioritized, and connected to action.

testimonials.name: Adaeze O.
testimonials.quote: Temitayo rebuilt our delivery pipeline from a brittle weekly release into a 40-per-week GitOps engine. The team finally trusts production again, and our weekends came back.

testimonials.name: Marcus L.
testimonials.quote: He cut our AWS spend by ~$18K/month in the first quarter without a single performance regression. Rare to find an engineer who blends FinOps discipline with deep platform skills.

testimonials.name: Priya S.
testimonials.quote: Our MTTR dropped under 30 minutes after Temitayo redesigned the observability stack. Runbooks became real, alerts became actionable, and incident reviews became evidence-driven.

testimonials.name: Daniel K.
testimonials.quote: The bare-metal Kubernetes platform he designed for us reads like a textbook on production discipline, GitOps, Vault, External Secrets, Velero, Kyverno, every layer thought through.

testimonials.name: Sarah M.
testimonials.quote: Temitayo is the rare engineer who can ship reliable infrastructure and explain it to auditors, executives, and junior engineers in the same week. SOC 2 evidence packets practically wrote themselves.

testimonials.name: Jonas R.
testimonials.quote: His AI-assisted ops workflow gave us evidence-grounded triage with human approval gates baked in, exactly the safety posture we needed before letting LLMs near production.
